original_hypothesis.title: Unbounded loop DoS via attacker-inflated staker list impacting any staker removal
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The rollup maintains `_stakerList` and `_stakerMap` and uses swap-and-pop deletion for removals, but there is no explicit cap on staker count. If validator whitelist is disabled (or becomes disabled), an attacker can create many stakers and bloat `_stakerList`. While core removals are O(1), any offchain or onchain logic that iterates stakers (not shown here) becomes expensive. Even within provided code, `getStakerAddress(uint64)` exposes the list; potential future/admin logic may iterate and become unusable, creating operational DoS. This hypothesis focuses on the presence of an ever-growing staker set without pruning constraints.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a common production failure mode: attacker uses permissionless entry to create large participant sets, making maintenance, monitoring, or any iteration-based function unusable. Even if current functions are O(1), the contract exposes the staker list publicly and other modules (not in provided scope) may rely on bounded staker sets.
deployment_assumptions: validatorWhitelistDisabled becomes true (via AFK/fork functions or initial config)
There exists (in full system) any onchain pathway iterating over stakers or using stakerCount() in loops (admin logic, reader, or external tooling affecting critical ops)
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: RollupUserLogic.sol exposes removeWhitelistAfterFork()/removeWhitelistAfterValidatorAfk() which can set validatorWhitelistDisabled=true; no staker cap visible in provided code.

proof_of_signal.lines: createNewStake(): pushes to _stakerList; no cap/pruning besides per-staker withdrawal; stakerCount() returns _stakerList.length
proof_of_signal.pattern: unbounded participant set growth + public indexing API

preconditions: Attacker can pass onlyValidator (whitelist disabled or attacker is validator)
Attacker has capital to create many staker entries
At least one critical pathway in the full protocol/tooling iterates over stakers (to be checked)

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: RollupCore.sol createNewStake() and public getters over _stakerList
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: ecosystem components may rely on bounded staker set; chain can be spammed with staker entries
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no stake-entry throttling besides stake amount; no cap

analysis_questions: Do any in-scope or adjacent contracts (admin logic, validators utils, readers) iterate over stakers onchain?
Are there protocol-critical calls that become infeasible when stakerCount is large?
Is there any economic deterrent sufficient to prevent staker-set bloat under current stake token economics?
validation_requirements: Search codebase for loops over stakerCount/getStakerAddress
Benchmark gas for any such looped functions under large stakerCount
Model attacker cost vs operational impact

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: After whitelist is disabled, attacker creates thousands of staker entries (each with minimum required stake) to bloat the staker set. Any onchain iteration-based maintenance/cleanup (if present elsewhere) becomes uncallable, stalling operational processes.
prerequisites: Significant capital to open many stakes

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Indirect but material via protocol liveness/maintenance failures
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All participants if key functions become uncallable
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Would likely require upgrade or admin intervention; otherwise persists

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Missing stress tests for large stakerCount and any iteration-based functions

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: N * baseStake for N fake stakers
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: monitor stakerCount growth and correlation with failing txs due to out-of-gas

validation_result.impact_summary: No DoS vulnerability exists in the core contracts as staker management uses O(1) operations and does not iterate over the staker list.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis suggests that an attacker could bloat `_stakerList` by creating many stakers, causing a Denial of Service for any logic that iterates over the list. However, a review of the in-scope contracts (`RollupCore.sol`, `RollupUserLogic.sol`, `EdgeChallengeManager.sol`) reveals that there is no on-chain iteration over `_stakerList`. The protocol correctly implements O(1) operations for adding stakers (`createNewStake` uses `push`) and removing stakers (`deleteStaker` uses the swap-and-pop pattern). The exposure of the list via `getStakerAddress(uint64)` and `stakerCount()` allows external callers to read the data in O(1) time per call, which is standard practice and does not introduce an on-chain gas limit DoS. Any off-chain tooling or hypothetical future on-chain logic would be responsible for paginating or handling large lists appropriately. Furthermore, creating a staker requires locking `baseStake` (which is typically >= 1 ETH), making list bloat economically expensive. Since the core protocol does not contain any vulnerable loops, the code is not defective.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code explicitly uses the swap-and-pop pattern in `deleteStaker` (RollupCore.sol:226-234) to ensure O(1) removals, demonstrating that the developers anticipated and mitigated array iteration gas issues. There are no loops over `_stakerList` in the provided codebase.

execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: No vulnerable path exists. `createNewStake` pushes to the array in O(1) time, and `deleteStaker` removes elements using swap-and-pop in O(1) time.

deployment_assumptions_checked: There exists (in full system) any onchain pathway iterating over stakers or using stakerCount() in loops (admin logic, reader, or external tooling affecting critical ops)
config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts or provided contracts show any on-chain iteration over the staker list.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario relies on the existence of an on-chain iteration over the staker list. Since no such iteration exists in the core protocol, the attacker cannot cause an out-of-gas DoS on critical protocol functions. The core protocol remains fully functional regardless of the staker list size.
validation_result.impact_assessment: None. The protocol is resilient against staker list bloat due to its O(1) state management.
uncertainties: Whether any out-of-scope contracts (like RollupAdminLogic) iterate over the staker list, though this would be an anti-pattern in those contracts rather than a defect in RollupCore.
